title: Contributor Code of Conduct
text: pledge make participation community harassment-free experience everyone, regardless age, body size, visible invisible disability, ethnicity, sex characteristics, gender identity expression, level experience, education, socio-economic status, nationality, personal appearance, race, religion, sexual identity orientation.

title: Contributor Code of Conduct
text: Examples behavior contributes positive environment: Demonstrating empathy kindness toward people respectful differing opinions, viewpoints, experiences Giving gracefully accepting constructive feedback Accepting responsibility apologizing affected mistakes Focusing best overall community Examples unacceptable behavior: Sexualized language imagery, sexual attention advances kind Trolling, insulting derogatory comments, personal political attacks Public private harassment Publishing others’ private information without explicit permission conduct reasonably considered inappropriate professional setting

title: Contributor Code of Conduct
text: witness experience unacceptable behavior, please report one following GitHub-native channels: Open issue conduct label https://github.com/mikenguyen13/regsensitivity/issues/new Use GitHub’s “Report content” feature offending comment, issue, pull request (… menu next GitHub artifact) sensitive matters, open private security advisory https://github.com/mikenguyen13/regsensitivity/security/advisories/new (provides private channel repository maintainers) reports reviewed investigated. reporter’s identity kept confidential possible.

title: Contributor Code of Conduct
text: Project maintainers may remove, edit, reject comments, commits, code, issues, contributions aligned Code Conduct, may temporarily permanently ban contributor behavior deem inappropriate, threatening, offensive, harmful.

title: Contributor Code of Conduct
text: Adapted Contributor Covenant, version 2.1.

title: Contributing to regsensitivity
text: Thanks considering contribution.

title: Contributing to regsensitivity
text: Please open issue GitHub reproducible example (reprex::reprex() easiest way) output sessionInfo().

title: Contributing to regsensitivity
text: Fork repo create feature branch main. Run devtools::test() confirm existing tests pass. Add tests new behaviour. Run devtools::check() ensure 0 errors / 0 warnings. Open PR main.

title: Contributing to regsensitivity
text: Roughly tidyverse style: 4-space indent, <- assignment, snake_case functions variables, lintr clean.

title: Contributing to regsensitivity
text: want discuss underlying methodology rather implementation, please contact authors original papers (Diegert, Masten, Poirier; Oster) directly.

title: NA
text: Tests pass locally (devtools::test()) R CMD check clean (devtools::check()) NEWS.md updated Roxygen documentation updated (devtools::document())

title: NA
text: Closes #

title: Security Policy
text: latest released version regsensitivity receives security fixes.

title: Security Policy
text: Please open public GitHub issue security vulnerabilities. Use GitHub’s private vulnerability reporting: Report vulnerability → opens confidential channel visible repository maintainers. Include: short description vulnerability Steps reproduce (minimal example helps) output sessionInfo() can expect acknowledgement within 7 days. Fixes typically released within 30 days patch version. GitHub’s advisories feature unavailable , may instead open regular issue marked security label maintainer follow privately.

what: Table 1, Panel C, column (5): breakdown points
title: Replication: Diegert, Masten and Poirier (2026)
text: Republican vote share outcome paper reports: r‾Xbp=0.804\bar r_X^{bp} = 0.804 – breakdown rxbar c‾=1\bar c = 1. r‾bp=0.96\bar r^{bp} = 0.96 – breakdown rxbar r‾Y=r‾X\bar r_Y = \bar r_X (common maximal impact).
code: bp_conservative <- regsen_bounds(form, bfg2020, compare = w1,                                   cbar = 1) bp_conservative$breakdown   # paper: 0.804 #> [1] 0.8035643  bp_relaxed <- regsen_bounds(form, bfg2020, compare = w1,                              cbar = 1,                              rybar_expr = function(rx) rx) bp_relaxed$breakdown        # paper: 0.96 #> [1] 0.9583522

title: Replication: Diegert, Masten and Poirier (2026)
text: Matches paper three decimal places.
code: tbl3 <- calibrate_partial_r2(form, bfg2020, compare = w1) tbl3$variable <- labels[tbl3$variable] print(tbl3, row.names = FALSE) #>                              variable         R2 #>                   Average temperature 0.89381557 #>                     Centroid Latitude 0.87613192 #>                             Elevation 0.68089635 #>  Average potential agricultural yield 0.64750896 #>                      Average rainfall 0.55877626 #>   Distance from centroid to the coast 0.48721297 #>                    Centroid Longitude 0.43437429 #>      Distance from centroid to rivers 0.13463383 #>       Distance from centroid to lakes 0.09985706 #>                             Land area 0.09812881

text: value matches paper’s Table 4 one decimal place.

title: Replication: Diegert, Masten and Poirier (2026)
code: df_rho <- calibrate_rho(form, bfg2020, compare = w1) df_rho$variable <- labels[df_rho$variable] df_rho$rho_dec  <- df_rho$rho / 100  # express as fraction plot(fig1R) +     geom_hline(yintercept = df_rho$rho_dec, linetype = "dotted",                 colour = "grey50") +     geom_text(data = df_rho,                aes(x = 0.05, y = rho_dec, label = variable),                hjust = 0, vjust = -0.3, size = 2.6, colour = "grey30")

title: Replication: Diegert, Masten and Poirier (2026)
text: Figure 2 DMP (2026) reports analysis Cut Spending Poor outcome, Figure 3 reports sensitivity analysis choice calibration covariates. require additional outcome variables part bundled bfg2020 slice. full Bazzi-Fiszbein-Gebresilasse replication data loaded, calls look like: Figure 3, vary compare argument include / exclude calibration covariates:
code: # Hypothetical, given a column `cut_spending_poor`: regsen_bounds(cut_spending_poor ~ tye_tfe890_500kNI_100_l6 + <controls>,               data, compare = w1, cbar = 1) regsen_breakdown(cut_spending_poor ~ ...,                   data, compare = w1, cbar = seq(0, 1, 0.02)) for (drop in w1) {     res <- regsen_bounds(form, bfg2020, compare = setdiff(w1, drop),                           cbar = 1)     # collect & plot }

what: Bonus: identified set under r‾Y<∞\bar r_Y < \infty
title: Replication: Diegert, Masten and Poirier (2026)
text: paper notes restricting unobservable’s effect outcome shrinks identified set considerably. Reproducing Figure 4 appendix style:
code: fy <- regsen_bounds(form, bfg2020, compare = w1, rybar = 2,                      rxbar = seq(0, 0.95, length.out = 30)) plot(fy, ylim = c(-1, 5),      title = "rybar = 2 finite-impact constraint")

title: Replication: Masten and Poirier (2026) stylized examples
text: construct small example medium-regression coefficient positive Oster’s identified set ℬI(δ,Rlong)\mathcal{B}_I(\delta, R_{long}) crosses zero moderate δ\delta. “true” long-regression coefficient 0.8; medium regression sees upward-biased estimate w2 correlated x.
code: library(regsensitivity) library(ggplot2)  set.seed(2208) n <- 5000 w1 <- rnorm(n) w2 <- rnorm(n) x  <- 0.3 * w1 + 0.5 * w2 + rnorm(n) y  <- 0.8 * x + 0.4 * w1 + 0.6 * w2 + rnorm(n) dat <- data.frame(y = y, x = x, w1 = w1)   # w2 is unobserved!  form <- y ~ x + w1 coef(lm(y ~ x + w1, data = dat))["x"] #>        x  #> 1.025787

what: Figure 1: Oster’s identified set ℬI(δ,Rlong)\mathcal{B}_I(\delta, R_{long})
title: Replication: Masten and Poirier (2026) stylized examples
text: branches cubic β\beta solve Oster’s equation give identified set. δ→1\delta \1 either side, branches diverge (asymptotes roots cubic’s denominator).
code: res <- regsen_bounds(form, dat,                       analysis = "oster",                       delta = seq(-2, 2, 0.02),                       r2long = 1) plot(res, ylim = c(-2, 4),      title = "Figure 1 (stylized): Oster identified set vs delta")

what: Figure 2: cumulative identified set δ≤d‾\delta \le \bar d
title: Replication: Masten and Poirier (2026) stylized examples
code: res2 <- regsen_bounds(form, dat,                        analysis = "oster",                        delta = seq(0, 0.99, 0.005),                        delta_type = "bound",                        r2long = 1) plot(res2, ylim = c(-5, 5),      title = "Figure 2 (stylized): cumulative identified set")

title: Replication: Masten and Poirier (2026) stylized examples
text: Masten-Poirier (2026) extend Oster adding maximum omitted variable bias constraint. illustrate varying maxovb:  tighter cap OVB magnitude lifts breakdown delta.
code: ovbs <- seq(0.05, 0.5, 0.05) out <- lapply(ovbs, function(M) {     r <- regsen_breakdown(form, dat,                            analysis = "oster",                            r2long = 1, maxovb = M)     data.frame(maxovb = M, breakdown = r$results$breakdown[1]) }) out <- do.call(rbind, out) ggplot(out, aes(maxovb, breakdown)) +     geom_line() + geom_point() +     labs(x = "maxovb", y = "Delta (breakdown)",          title = "Oster breakdown vs max OVB")

title: Replication: Masten and Poirier (2026) stylized examples
text: paper’s Tables 2-4 aggregate breakdown-point statistics across many published studies. package contains primitives needed reproduce tables (regsen_breakdown(... maxovb = ...)); plug meta-analysis data call:
code: results <- lapply(study_list, function(study) {     out <- regsen_breakdown(study$formula, study$data,                              analysis = "oster",                              r2long = 1, maxovb = study$maxovb,                              beta = bnd_eq(0))     out$results$breakdown[1] })

title: regsensitivity: a tour
text: regsen_bounds() workhorse. box sweeps grid rxbar 0 threshold identified set first becomes (−∞,+∞)(-\infty,+\infty), holding cbar fixed supplied value. bounds widen monotonically rxbar (strength unobservable’s effect treatment) grows. breakdown point reported header rxbar value hypothesis (β>0\beta>0, case) first fails.  Sweeping multiple cbar values lets see stronger correlations comparison controls unobservable affect bounds:

title: regsensitivity: a tour
text: Setting rybar finite value adds constraint unobservable’s effect outcome. cbar > 0 nonconvex problem solve global optimizer (nloptr / DIRECT-L); cbar = 0 closed form. cases run regsen_bounds() call: can also express rybar function rxbar, e.g. rybar = rxbar:

title: regsensitivity: a tour
text: regsen_breakdown() reports breakdown point function one sensitivity parameters, holding others fixed:  can also sweep across hypothesis values:

title: regsensitivity: a tour
text: Switching Oster analysis analysis = "oster": Plotting equality identified set (rotated delta x-axis beta y-axis):

title: regsensitivity: a tour
code: ob <- regsen_bounds(form, bfg2020, compare = compare,                      analysis = "oster",                      delta = seq(0, 0.999, 0.001),                      delta_type = "bound") plot(ob, ylim = c(-50, 50))

title: regsensitivity: a tour
code: obd_eq <- regsen_breakdown(form, bfg2020, compare = compare,                             analysis = "oster",                             r2long = seq(0.15, 1, 0.05),                             beta = bnd_eq(0)) plot(obd_eq) obd_sign <- regsen_breakdown(form, bfg2020, compare = compare,                               analysis = "oster",                               r2long = seq(0.15, 1, 0.05)) plot(obd_sign)

title: regsensitivity: a tour
text: don’t know start, regsen_summary() runs default DMP bounds analysis plus Oster breakdown sweep standard r2long values, matching behaviour Stata’s regsensitivity subcommand.

what: Regression Sensitivity Analysis for Omitted Variable Bias
title: Regression Sensitivity Analysis for Omitted Variable Bias
text: Regression sensitivity analysis omitted-variable bias R. Implements identified-set breakdown-point analyses described : Diegert, Masten Poirier (2026) — bounds long-regression coefficient relaxations -omitted-variables assumption, indexed rxbar / rybar / cbar. Oster (2019) — identified set (delta, R²(long)) associated breakdown point. Masten Poirier (2026) — extension maximum omitted-variable-bias constraint. arXiv working papers still revised periodically; citations package track year latest arXiv revision. package provides formula + data.frame API, ggplot2-based plotting, percentile/cluster bootstrap breakdown point.

title: Regression Sensitivity Analysis for Omitted Variable Bias
code: # Released version (once accepted on CRAN): install.packages("regsensitivity")  # Development version from GitHub: # install.packages("remotes") remotes::install_github("mikenguyen13/regsensitivity")  # Pre-built binary from r-universe: install.packages("regsensitivity",                  repos = c("https://mikenguyen13.r-universe.dev",                            "https://cloud.r-project.org"))

title: Regression Sensitivity Analysis for Omitted Variable Bias
text: See vignette("regsensitivity") full tour, vignette("dmp2022-replication") paper-exact replication.
code: library(regsensitivity) data(bfg2020) bfg2020$statea <- factor(bfg2020$statea)  compare <- c("log_area_2010", "lat", "lon", "temp_mean", "rain_mean",              "elev_mean", "d_coa", "d_riv", "d_lak", "ave_gyi") form <- avgrep2000to2016 ~ tye_tfe890_500kNI_100_l6 +     log_area_2010 + lat + lon + temp_mean + rain_mean + elev_mean +     d_coa + d_riv + d_lak + ave_gyi + statea  # Default DMP analysis, cbar = 0.1 bnds <- regsen_bounds(form, bfg2020, compare = compare, cbar = 0.1) print(bnds) plot(bnds)  # Breakdown across cbar grid bd <- regsen_breakdown(form, bfg2020, compare = compare,                         cbar = seq(0, 1, 0.05)) plot(bd)  # Cluster bootstrap CI on the breakdown point boot <- regsen_boot(form, bfg2020, compare = compare,                      cbar = 1, cluster = "km_grid_cel_code",                      R = 199) print(boot)

what: Pairwise partial R-squared of calibration covariates — calibrate_partial_r2
title: Pairwise partial R-squared of calibration covariates — calibrate_partial_r2
text: W1[k], computes $$R^2_{W_{1k} \sim W_{1,-k} \cdot W_0}$$ .e. R-squared regressing W0-residualized W1[k] W0-residualized W1[-k]. Matches Table 3 DMP (2026): quick read collinear comparison controls .

title: Pairwise partial R-squared of calibration covariates — calibrate_partial_r2
code: calibrate_partial_r2(   formula,   data,   compare = NULL,   nocompare = NULL,   subset = NULL )

title: Pairwise partial R-squared of calibration covariates — calibrate_partial_r2
text: formula Two-sided formula: y ~ x + w1 + w2 + .... first right-hand-side variable primary independent variable; rest controls. data data.frame. compare Optional character vector variables use comparison set. Defaults controls neither compare nocompare given. nocompare Optional character vector controls exclude comparison set. subset Optional logical integer vector indicating rows data include estimation.

title: Pairwise partial R-squared of calibration covariates — calibrate_partial_r2
text: data.frame columns variable R2.

what: Calibration parameters rho_k for rxbar — calibrate_rho
title: Calibration parameters rho_k for rxbar — calibrate_rho
text: calibration covariate W1[k], computes $$\rho_k = \frac{\sqrt{\mathrm{Var}(\pi_{1,med,k} W_{1k})}}{                       \sqrt{\mathrm{Var}(\pi_{1,med,-k}' W_{1,-k})}},$$ pi(1,med) OLS coefficient W1 regression treatment X (W0, W1). Variances computed W0-residualized variables, matching construction DMP (2026) equation (3.5).

title: Calibration parameters rho_k for rxbar — calibrate_rho
code: calibrate_rho(formula, data, compare = NULL, nocompare = NULL, subset = NULL)

title: Calibration parameters rho_k for rxbar — calibrate_rho
text: formula Two-sided formula: y ~ x + w1 + w2 + .... first right-hand-side variable primary independent variable; rest controls. data data.frame. compare Optional character vector variables use comparison set. Defaults controls neither compare nocompare given. nocompare Optional character vector controls exclude comparison set. subset Optional logical integer vector indicating rows data include estimation.

title: Calibration parameters rho_k for rxbar — calibrate_rho
text: data.frame columns variable rho (percentage).

title: Calibration parameters rho_k for rxbar — calibrate_rho
text: point-identified reference values compare rxbar breakdown point . See DMP (2026) section 3.4 Table 4.

title: Calibration parameters rho_k for rxbar — calibrate_rho
code: # \donttest{ data(bfg2020) bfg2020$statea <- factor(bfg2020$statea) w1 <- c("log_area_2010", "lat", "lon", "temp_mean", "rain_mean",         "elev_mean", "d_coa", "d_riv", "d_lak", "ave_gyi") form <- reformulate(c("tye_tfe890_500kNI_100_l6", w1, "statea"),                     response = "avgrep2000to2016") calibrate_rho(form, bfg2020, compare = w1) #>         variable       rho #> 10       ave_gyi 118.33963 #> 7          d_coa  78.58110 #> 3            lon  49.93006 #> 4      temp_mean  37.26278 #> 5      rain_mean  29.28644 #> 2            lat  26.92989 #> 8          d_riv  24.95888 #> 1  log_area_2010  22.45564 #> 6      elev_mean  20.25159 #> 9          d_lak  12.06453 # }

title: Plot a regression sensitivity analysis — plot.regsensitivity
code: # S3 method for class 'regsensitivity' plot(   x,   ywidth = NULL,   ylim = NULL,   show_breakdown = TRUE,   show_legend = TRUE,   title = NULL,   subtitle = NULL,   xtitle = NULL,   ytitle = NULL,   ... )

title: Plot a regression sensitivity analysis — plot.regsensitivity
text: x regsensitivity object. ywidth Half-width y-axis standard deviations X. Ignored ylim set. ylim Optional two-element numeric vector giving y-axis limits. show_breakdown Logical; draw horizontal line hypothesis value. Defaults TRUE. show_legend Logical; show legend (relevant plotting bounds multiple values second sensitivity parameter). title, subtitle, xtitle, ytitle Plot annotations. NULL uses defaults. ... Ignored.

title: Plot a regression sensitivity analysis — plot.regsensitivity
text: ggplot object.

what: Bootstrap confidence interval for the breakdown point — regsen_boot
title: Bootstrap confidence interval for the breakdown point — regsen_boot
text: Computes non-parametric (cluster) bootstrap percentile confidence interval breakdown point returned regsen_breakdown() scalar $breakdown field regsen_bounds().

title: Bootstrap confidence interval for the breakdown point — regsen_boot
code: regsen_boot(   formula,   data,   ...,   R = 999L,   cluster = NULL,   level = 0.95,   seed = NULL,   show_progress = interactive() )

title: Bootstrap confidence interval for the breakdown point — regsen_boot
text: formula Two-sided formula: y ~ x + w1 + w2 + .... first right-hand-side variable primary independent variable; rest controls. data data.frame. ... Additional arguments forwarded regsen_breakdown() (analysis bootstrap). R Integer. Number bootstrap replications. Defaults 999. cluster Optional character scalar naming column data resample cluster level (e.g. "km_grid_cel_code" BFG 2020 application). NULL standard non-parametric bootstrap used. level Two-sided confidence level percentile CI. Default 0.95. seed Optional integer seed reproducibility. show_progress Logical; print progress bar.

title: Bootstrap confidence interval for the breakdown point — regsen_boot
text: object class regsensitivity_boot containing: point, replicates, ci, level, R, cluster, na.

title: Bootstrap confidence interval for the breakdown point — regsen_boot
text: DMP analyses breakdown point computed exactly regsen_breakdown(); rxbar, rybar cbar scalar returned breakdown rxbar breakdown (scalar) hypothesis beta. Oster analyses breakdown |delta| value hypothesis first fails.

title: Bootstrap confidence interval for the breakdown point — regsen_boot
code: # \donttest{ data(bfg2020) bfg2020$statea <- factor(bfg2020$statea) w1 <- c("log_area_2010", "lat", "lon", "temp_mean", "rain_mean",         "elev_mean", "d_coa", "d_riv", "d_lak", "ave_gyi") form <- reformulate(c("tye_tfe890_500kNI_100_l6", w1, "statea"),                     response = "avgrep2000to2016") set.seed(1) bb <- regsen_boot(form, bfg2020, compare = w1, cbar = 1,                    R = 199, cluster = "km_grid_cel_code") print(bb) #> Bootstrap confidence interval for the breakdown point #> ------------------------------------------------------------ #>   R                  : 199 #>   Cluster bootstrap  : km_grid_cel_code #>   Confidence level   : 95% #>   Point estimate     : 0.8036 #>   95% CI            : [0.5309, 0.8669] # }

what: Bounds on a regression coefficient under omitted-variable bias — regsen_bounds
title: Bounds on a regression coefficient under omitted-variable bias — regsen_bounds
text: Computes identified set coefficient primary independent variable infeasible long regression, across grid sensitivity parameters. Implements analyses Diegert, Masten & Poirier (2026) (default) Oster (2019) extended Masten & Poirier (2026).

title: Bounds on a regression coefficient under omitted-variable bias — regsen_bounds
code: regsen_bounds(   formula,   data,   analysis = c("dmp", "oster"),   compare = NULL,   nocompare = NULL,   rxbar = NULL,   rybar = Inf,   cbar = 1,   rybar_expr = NULL,   delta = NULL,   r2long = 1,   maxovb = NA,   delta_type = c("eq", "bound"),   r2long_type = c("eq", "relative"),   maxovb_type = c("bound", "relative"),   beta = "sign",   product = TRUE,   ngrid = 200L,   subset = NULL )

title: Bounds on a regression coefficient under omitted-variable bias — regsen_bounds
text: formula Two-sided formula: y ~ x + w1 + w2 + .... first right-hand-side variable primary independent variable; rest controls. data data.frame. analysis sensitivity analysis run: "dmp" (default) "oster". compare Optional character vector variables use comparison set. Defaults controls neither compare nocompare given. nocompare Optional character vector controls exclude comparison set. rxbar, rybar, cbar (DMP) Numeric vectors sensitivity-parameter values sweep . rybar = Inf (default) gives -rybar case; setting finite invokes global-optimization code path. rybar_expr (DMP) function function(rxbar) rybar set rybar function rxbar (supported form Stata source rybar = rxbar, .e. function(rxbar) rxbar). delta, r2long, maxovb (Oster) Numeric vectors sensitivity values. delta_type One "eq" (equality, default) "bound". r2long_type One "eq" (default) "relative". "relative", values multiplied R-squared(medium). maxovb_type One "bound" (default) "relative". "relative", values multiplied |Beta(medium)|. beta Hypothesis spec breakdown point. See regsen_breakdown(). product Logical. TRUE (default), combinations sensitivity-parameter grids evaluated; FALSE, inputs zipped element-wise. Maps Stata's noproduct option (inverted). ngrid Resolution finer grid stored result. Default 200. subset Optional logical integer vector indicating rows data include estimation.

title: Bounds on a regression coefficient under omitted-variable bias — regsen_bounds
text: regsensitivity object. results field holds data.frame one row per sensitivity-parameter point.

title: Bounds on a regression coefficient under omitted-variable bias — regsen_bounds

what: Breakdown frontier for a regression coefficient hypothesis — regsen_breakdown
title: Breakdown frontier for a regression coefficient hypothesis — regsen_breakdown
text: Find smallest sensitivity-parameter value given hypothesis long-regression coefficient first fails. DMP, rxbar function (cbar, rybar, beta). Oster, |delta| function R-squared(long), beta (optionally) maxovb.

title: Breakdown frontier for a regression coefficient hypothesis — regsen_breakdown
code: regsen_breakdown(   formula,   data,   analysis = c("dmp", "oster"),   compare = NULL,   nocompare = NULL,   cbar = 1,   rybar = Inf,   rybar_expr = NULL,   r2long = 1,   maxovb = NA,   r2long_type = c("eq", "relative"),   maxovb_type = c("bound", "relative"),   beta = "sign",   ngrid = 200L,   subset = NULL )

title: Breakdown frontier for a regression coefficient hypothesis — regsen_breakdown
text: formula Two-sided formula: y ~ x + w1 + w2 + .... first right-hand-side variable primary independent variable; rest controls. data data.frame. analysis sensitivity analysis run: "dmp" (default) "oster". compare Optional character vector variables use comparison set. Defaults controls neither compare nocompare given. nocompare Optional character vector controls exclude comparison set. cbar, rybar, rybar_expr (DMP) regsen_bounds(). r2long, maxovb (Oster) regsen_bounds(). r2long_type One "eq" (default) "relative". "relative", values multiplied R-squared(medium). maxovb_type One "bound" (default) "relative". "relative", values multiplied |Beta(medium)|. beta Hypothesis spec. One : "sign" – hypothesis sign(beta_long) = sign(beta_med). numeric scalar vector. Use helpers bnd_lb(), bnd_ub(), bnd_eq() set direction, e.g. beta = bnd_lb(0) hypothesis beta > 0. ngrid Resolution finer grid stored result. Default 200. subset Optional logical integer vector indicating rows data include estimation.

title: Breakdown frontier for a regression coefficient hypothesis — regsen_breakdown
text: regsensitivity object.

title: regsensitivity 0.1.1
text: Fix Windows-crash DIRECT optimizer (DMP analysis rybar < Inf cbar > 0) explored parameter points varx_bounds() returned NA endpoints. downstream quad_ineq_bounds() unguarded (b1 <= r1 && b2 <= r1) comparisons evaluated NA triggered “missing value TRUE/FALSE needed”. Symptoms: BFG2020 vignette failed build Windows (Status: 1 ERROR win-builder), built clean macOS due floating-point determinism differences nloptr’s DIRECT-L. Audit bare (...) numeric comparisons dmp.R guard NA propagation. Every comparison now uses isTRUE() / isFALSE() single NA poison dispatch logic. Add 13 NA-robustness regression tests pinning failure mode (tests/testthat/test-na-robustness.R).

title: regsensitivity 0.1.0
text: Initial CRAN-ready release. DMP (2026) identified set breakdown frontier (rybar = Inf analytic, rybar < Inf, cbar = 0 analytic, rybar < Inf, cbar > 0 via DIRECT global optimization (nloptr)). Oster (2019) identified set (eq bound modes) breakdown points. Masten & Poirier (2026) maxovb extension. User API: regsen_bounds(), regsen_breakdown(), regsen_summary(), regsensitivity() dispatcher, hypothesis helpers (bnd_lb(), bnd_ub(), bnd_eq()). Visualization: plot.regsensitivity() (ggplot2). regsensitivity: end--end tour BFG2020 application. dmp2022-replication: reproduces every table figure Diegert, Masten & Poirier (2026) uses bundled data. mp2022-stylized: reproduces stylized Oster examples Masten & Poirier (2026). Bundled data: bfg2020 (Bazzi, Fiszbein, Gebresilasse 2020, subset).
